regulator: FINMA (Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority); Swiss National Bank (SNB); Federal Council / Federal Department of Finance (FDF) for the pending FinIA amendment
instruments: Federal Act on Banks and Savings Banks (Banking Act, BankA/BankG), SR 952.0 -- the general deposit-taking licence requirement (Art. 1) that captures many crypto and stablecoin business models today; not a crypto-specific statute

Federal Act on Banks and Savings Banks, Art. 1b, and the Banking Ordinance (BankO/BankV) provisions implementing it -- the 'FinTech licence', in force since 1 January 2019, permitting acceptance of public deposits or cryptobased assets up to CHF 100 million, provided they are not invested and no interest is paid on them; minimum capital of the greater of CHF 300,000 or 3% of public deposits held; the current innovation-tier authorisation route for non-bank digital-asset businesses

FINMA press release, 'FINMA grants first licences for blockchain companies' (26 August 2019) -- first-time grant of banking and securities dealer licences to SEBA Crypto AG (Zug) and Sygnum AG (Zurich)

FINMA Guidance 06/2024 on Stablecoins (26 July 2024) -- addresses when a stablecoin issuer's use of a bank default guarantee removes the need for the issuer itself to hold a FINMA banking licence, and the resulting risk this places on the guaranteeing bank; full treatment is in the Stablecoins pillar and cross-referenced here rather than duplicated

Federal Council / FDF: consultation draft amending the Federal Act on Financial Institutions (FinIA/FINIG) to abolish the Art. 1b fintech licence and replace it with two new licence categories, 'payment instrument institutions' and 'crypto-institutions' (consultation opened 22 October 2025, closed 6 February 2026) -- not enacted, and no dispatch (Botschaft) to the Federal Assembly identified as adopted as of this research; full treatment of the stablecoin-issuance aspects of this proposal is in the Stablecoins pillar and cross-referenced here rather than duplicated

Swiss National Bank: Project Helvetia -- pilot issuance of wholesale central bank digital currency (wholesale CBDC) on the SIX Digital Exchange platform, most recently extended and expanded per the SNB press release of 30 June 2025

Swiss National Bank: 'Will the Swiss franc soon only exist digitally?' (snb.ch/en/snb-explained/cbdc) -- the SNB's own published position distinguishing wholesale CBDC (actively piloted) from retail CBDC (no stated advantage currently seen)
standing_summary: There is no dedicated Swiss statute for banking or money-related crypto activity; this pillar covers how FINMA applies the existing, technology-neutral Banking Act to deposit-taking and bank licensing involving digital assets, how the Federal Council proposes to amend that framework's innovation tier, and how the Swiss National Bank (SNB) treats digital central-bank money -- the same amend-and-apply pattern that runs through every pillar of this jurisdiction. Deposit-taking and the fintech licence: the Banking Act (BankA/BankG, SR 952.0) requires a banking licence for anyone accepting public deposits on a commercial basis, a general rule that many stablecoin and other crypto-asset structures trigger today depending on how they are structured (the mechanics of when a stablecoin triggers this are tracked in the Stablecoins pillar). Since 1 January 2019, Art. 1b of the Banking Act has provided a lighter-touch 'FinTech licence' for institutions accepting public deposits, including cryptobased assets, up to CHF 100 million, on condition that the deposits are not invested and no interest is paid on them; FINMA's own guidance states that FinTech-licence holders must be a company limited by shares, a corporation with unlimited partners or a limited liability company registered and operating in Switzerland, must hold minimum capital equal to the greater of CHF 300,000 or 3% of public deposits held, and that in the event of the institution's bankruptcy 'client assets are neither privileged nor protected by deposit protection' under the ordinary bank depositor-protection scheme. This is the current innovation-tier route for non-bank digital-asset businesses that do not seek a full banking licence. Two FINMA-licensed crypto banks: on 26 August 2019, FINMA announced that it had, for the first time, granted full banking and securities dealer licences to two 'pure-play blockchain service providers' -- SEBA Crypto AG, registered in Zug, and Sygnum AG, registered in Zurich -- to offer services to institutional and professional clients, with FINMA's release noting that 'various conditions are attached, ensuring that the businesses are set up in an orderly manner.' SEBA Crypto AG subsequently rebranded to AMINA Bank AG (per the company's own December 2023 announcement and contemporaneous press coverage; this specific rebranding fact was not independently re-confirmed against a FINMA press release in this research pass, see open items). Both Sygnum Bank AG and AMINA Bank AG continue to describe themselves publicly as FINMA-licensed banks and securities firms, but this research did not independently re-verify their current authorisation status against FINMA's live public register of authorised institutions in this pass; that verification step is flagged as an open item rather than asserted as independently confirmed. Pending replacement of the fintech licence: on 22 October 2025 the Federal Council, via the FDF, opened a public consultation on an amendment to the Federal Act on Financial Institutions (FinIA/FINIG) that would abolish the Art. 1b fintech licence entirely and replace it, within FinIA, with two new licence categories -- 'payment instrument institutions', which the consultation materials describe as removing the existing CHF 100 million deposit cap and requiring segregation of client funds, among other conditions (full detail on the stablecoin-issuance permissions of this category is in the Stablecoins pillar); and 'crypto-institutions', a separate category for other crypto-asset services with licensing criteria modelled on, but lighter than, those for securities firms. The consultation closed on 6 February 2026; this research found no confirmation that the Federal Council has since adopted and submitted a dispatch (Botschaft) to the Federal Assembly, so the proposal remains at consultation-outcome-pending status only, is not enacted, and this pillar makes no prediction about its eventual form or timing. Until and unless it becomes law, the Art. 1b fintech licence described above remains the current, in-force innovation-tier route. Swiss National Bank -- wholesale CBDC: the SNB's Project Helvetia pilots the issuance of wholesale central bank digital currency -- tokenised Swiss franc central-bank money reserved for financial institutions -- on the regulated, DLT-based SIX Digital Exchange (SDX) platform. Per the SNB's press release of 30 June 2025, the SNB decided to extend the pilot for a further year, continuing 'until at least mid-2027,' and to expand it to include settlement of tokenised assets using an RTGS link to the SNB's SIC payment system as an additional approach alongside the existing SDX-based wholesale CBDC, so the two settlement approaches can be compared in a production environment; the SNB's release states explicitly that the extension 'does not constitute a commitment on the part of the SNB to introduce wholesale CBDC on a permanent basis.' Swiss National Bank -- retail CBDC: the SNB's own published position, stated on its 'Will the Swiss franc soon only exist digitally?' explainer page, is that 'the SNB currently does not see any clear advantages for the general public in its issuance of a digital franc,' distinguishing this from its more exploratory stance toward wholesale CBDC, where it states it 'believed early on that digital base money, especially in the form of wholesale CBDC, could offer added value.' This pillar records that stated position only, with no assessment of whether it is likely to change and no prediction about SNB policy. This pillar covers the federal, FINMA/SNB level; it does not track cantonal-level matters, which are not a significant feature of Swiss banking-and-money crypto regulation and are out of scope.

key_links.label: FINMA: FinTech licence (authorisation category page, Art. 1b Banking Act)

open_items: SEBA Crypto AG's rebranding to AMINA Bank AG (announced by the company, effective December 2023) was corroborated only against the company's own announcement and contemporaneous press coverage, not against a distinct FINMA press release confirming the licensed entity's name change; this should be re-checked against FINMA's own register before being treated as fully verified.

Neither Sygnum Bank AG's nor AMINA Bank AG's current FINMA banking/securities-dealer authorisation status was independently re-verified against FINMA's live public register of authorised institutions (finma.ch/en/finma-public/authorised-institutions-individuals-and-products/) in this research pass, which requires a name-search lookup or a downloadable Excel/PDF list this research could not machine-query directly; both firms' own public materials state they remain FINMA-licensed as of this research, but that is a claim by the regulated entities, not independent confirmation against the regulator's own current register.

The Federal Council's FinIA amendment consultation closed on 6 February 2026. This research found no primary Federal Council or FDF statement confirming whether a dispatch (Botschaft) has since been adopted and submitted to the Federal Assembly, nor any officially stated target date for parliamentary consideration or entry into force; secondary commentary suggests entry into force is not expected before 2027, but this could not be verified against a primary source and is not asserted as fact.

The stablecoin-issuance permissions and duties (redemption, backing, segregation) of the proposed 'payment instrument institution' licence category, and the AML/CFT duties applicable to stablecoin issuers, are tracked in full in the Stablecoins and AML/CFT & Enforcement pillars respectively for this jurisdiction; this pillar cross-references rather than duplicates that research.

The DLT Act's Banking Act and bankruptcy-law amendments concerning custody and segregation of crypto-based assets held for clients are tracked in full in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar for this jurisdiction; this pillar cross-references rather than duplicates that research.

FINMA Guidance 06/2024's exact minimum requirements for bank default guarantees used by stablecoin issuers to avoid triggering a banking licence are stated here based on the FINMA press release announcing the guidance and are tracked in full detail in the Stablecoins pillar; the underlying guidance PDF's complete text was not independently re-parsed in this research pass.

This pillar covers the federal (FINMA/SNB) level only; it does not track any cantonal-level involvement in crypto-related banking matters, which was not found to be a materially significant feature of the Swiss regime in this research pass.
